annotations.label: Square root

anchor.snippet: a square root of a number x is a number y such that

annotations.note: `IsSquare a` is defined as `∃ r, a = r * r`, capturing the abstract notion that `a` has a square root.

annotations.label: Square roots of 16

anchor.snippet: For example, 4 and −4 are square roots of 16

annotations.note: No specific worked example computing the square roots of 16 is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Principal square root

anchor.snippet: Every nonnegative real number x has a unique nonnegative square root, called the principal square root

annotations.note: `Real.sqrt` (with `Real.sqrt_nonneg` and `Real.sq_sqrt`) realizes the principal nonnegative square root.

annotations.label: Principal square root of 9

anchor.snippet: to express the fact that the principal square root of 9 is 3

annotations.note: There is no dedicated lemma stating `√9 = 3` (though `norm_num` can evaluate it).

annotations.label: Two square roots of a positive number

anchor.snippet: Every positive number x has two square roots

annotations.note: `sq_eq_sq_iff_eq_or_eq_neg` shows in any domain that `a² = b²` iff `a = ±b`, so each positive real has exactly two real square roots.

annotations.label: Square root function

anchor.snippet: is a function that maps the set of nonnegative real numbers onto itself

annotations.note: `Real.sqrt : ℝ → ℝ` (with `Real.sqrt_nonneg`) is the principal-branch square root function.

annotations.label: Rationality of square roots

anchor.snippet: The square root of x is rational if and only if x is a rational number that can be represented as a ratio of two perfect squares

annotations.note: `irrational_sqrt_ratCast_iff_of_nonneg` (and its `IsSquare`-based variants) characterizes when `√q` is rational.

annotations.label: Square root of x squared equals absolute value

anchor.snippet: For all real numbers x

annotations.note: `Real.sqrt_sq_eq_abs` states `√(x^2) = |x|` for all real `x`.

annotations.label: Multiplicativity of square roots

anchor.snippet: For all nonnegative real numbers x and y

annotations.note: `Real.sqrt_mul` (and `NNReal.sqrt_mul`) gives `√(xy) = √x · √y` for `0 ≤ x`.

anchors.snippet: For all nonnegative real numbers x and y

annotations.label: Continuity and differentiability of square root

anchor.snippet: The square root function is continuous for all nonnegative x , and differentiable for all positive x

annotations.note: `Real.continuous_sqrt` gives global continuity and `HasDerivAt.sqrt` (Mathlib.Analysis.SpecialFunctions.Sqrt) handles differentiability where the argument is nonzero.

anchors.snippet: The square root function is continuous for all nonnegative x , and differentiable for all positive x

annotations.label: Taylor series of square root

anchor.snippet: converges for | x | ≤ 1

annotations.note: No explicit power-series expansion of `√(1+x)` with its radius of convergence is recorded in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Square roots of positive integers are quadratic integers

anchor.snippet: The square roots of an integer are algebraic integers

annotations.note: Mathlib has the general framework of `IsIntegral`/algebraic integers, but no specific lemma stating that `√n` (for `n : ℤ`) is an algebraic/quadratic integer.

annotations.label: Square root of a prime factorization

anchor.snippet: the square root of a positive integer is the product of the roots of its prime factors

annotations.note: No lemma expresses `√n` as the product of square roots over the prime factorization of `n`.

anchors.snippet: the square root of a positive integer is the product of the roots of its prime factors

annotations.label: Perfect squares have integer square roots

anchor.snippet: The square roots of the perfect squares (e.g., 0, 1, 4, 9, 16) are integers

annotations.note: `Nat.sqrt_eq` gives `Nat.sqrt (n*n) = n`, and `IsSquare` characterizes perfect squares whose `Real.sqrt` is a natural number.

annotations.label: Non-perfect-square positive integers have irrational square roots

anchor.section: As expansions in other numeral systems
anchor.snippet: the square roots of positive integers are irrational numbers , and therefore have non-repeating digits

annotations.note: `irrational_sqrt_natCast_iff` states `Irrational (√n) ↔ ¬IsSquare n`.

annotations.label: Lagrange's theorem on periodic continued fractions

anchor.snippet: Lagrange found that the representation of the square root of any non-square positive integer as a continued fraction is periodic

annotations.note: Mathlib has a general `ContinuedFractions` framework but does not contain Lagrange's periodicity theorem for square-root continued fractions.

annotations.label: Continued fraction of square root of 11

anchor.snippet: the simple continued fraction for the square root of 11

annotations.note: No concrete computation of the continued fraction of √11 appears in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: the simple continued fraction for the square root of 11

annotations.label: Babylonian/Heron's method

anchor.snippet: The most common iterative method of square root calculation by hand is known as the " Babylonian method "

annotations.note: `Nat.sqrt` uses a Newton-style iteration internally, but the Babylonian/Heron real-valued recurrence is not formalized as a numerical algorithm with proved properties.

annotations.label: Quadratic convergence of Babylonian method

anchor.snippet: If a is positive, the convergence is quadratic

annotations.note: No analysis of the Babylonian iteration's quadratic order of convergence is present.

annotations.label: Imaginary unit

anchor.section: Square roots of negative and complex numbers
anchor.snippet: called the imaginary unit , which is defined such that

annotations.note: `Complex.I` is the imaginary unit with `Complex.I_sq : I^2 = -1`.

anchors.section: Square roots of negative and complex numbers
anchors.snippet: called the imaginary unit , which is defined such that

anchor.section: Square roots of negative and complex numbers
anchor.snippet: if x is any nonnegative number, then the principal square root of − x is

annotations.note: `Complex.sqrt_neg_of_nonneg` evaluates `Complex.sqrt (-a) = I * √a.re` for nonneg `a`.

anchors.snippet: if x is any nonnegative number, then the principal square root of − x is

annotations.label: Two square roots of a nonzero complex number

anchor.section: Square roots of negative and complex numbers
anchor.snippet: For every non-zero complex number z there exist precisely two numbers w such that

annotations.note: Over a domain (in particular `ℂ`), `w² = z` has solutions `±w₀`, so a nonzero complex number has exactly two square roots.

annotations.label: Principal square root of a complex number

anchor.section: Principal square root of a complex number
anchor.snippet: then the principal square root of

annotations.note: `Complex.sqrt a := a ^ (2⁻¹ : ℂ)` defines the principal complex square root.

anchors.section: Principal square root of a complex number
anchors.snippet: then the principal square root of

annotations.label: Holomorphy of principal square root

anchor.section: Principal square root of a complex number
anchor.snippet: The principal square root function is holomorphic everywhere except on the set of non-positive real numbers

annotations.note: `Complex.differentiableOn_sqrt` proves `Complex.sqrt` is differentiable on the slit plane (the complement of the non-positive reals).

annotations.label: Algebraic formula for principal square root

anchor.snippet: the following formula can be used for the principal square root

annotations.note: `Complex.sqrt_eq_real_add_ite` expresses `Complex.sqrt a` as `√((‖a‖+a.re)/2) ± I·√((‖a‖-a.re)/2)`.

annotations.label: Principal square roots of ±i

anchor.snippet: the principal square roots of ± i are given by

annotations.note: `Complex.sqrt_I` and `Complex.sqrt_neg_I` evaluate `sqrt I` and `sqrt (-I)`.

annotations.label: Cube root

anchor.section: n th roots and polynomial roots
anchor.snippet: A cube root of

annotations.note: No dedicated `cbrt` definition is present; `Real.rpow x (1/3)` is used ad hoc when needed.

annotations.label: n-th root

anchor.section: n th roots and polynomial roots
anchor.snippet: If n is an integer greater than two, a n -th root of

annotations.note: There is no general `nthRoot` function; nonneg n-th roots are realized via `rpow (1/n)` and roots of unity are formalized as `rootsOfUnity`.

annotations.label: Root of a polynomial

anchor.section: n th roots and polynomial roots
anchor.snippet: Given any polynomial p , a root of p is a number y such that

annotations.note: `Polynomial.IsRoot p a` is defined as `p.eval a = 0`.

annotations.label: Abel–Ruffini theorem

anchor.section: n th roots and polynomial roots
anchor.snippet: Abel–Ruffini theorem states that, in general, the roots of a polynomial of degree five or higher cannot be expressed in terms of n th roots

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes one direction (solvable-by-radicals ⇒ solvable Galois group); the explicit `Equation.quintic` example/witness needed for the full Abel–Ruffini statement is not present.

annotations.label: Unique positive-definite square root

anchor.section: Square roots of matrices and operators
anchor.snippet: If A is a positive-definite matrix or operator, then there exists precisely one positive definite matrix or operator B

annotations.note: `CFC.sqrt` together with `CFC.sqrt_unique` (and `Matrix.PosSemidef.sqrt` for matrices) provides the unique nonnegative operator square root.

annotations.label: Infinitely many square roots of the identity matrix

anchor.section: Square roots of matrices and operators
anchor.snippet: the 2 × 2 identity matrix has an infinity of square roots

annotations.note: No example exhibiting the infinitely many involutory square roots of the 2×2 identity is present.

annotations.label: At most two square roots in an integral domain

anchor.snippet: Each element of an integral domain has no more than 2 square roots

annotations.note: `sq_eq_sq_iff_eq_or_eq_neg` (in a no-zero-divisors ring) shows `a² = b²` iff `a = ±b`, so at most two square roots.

annotations.label: Square roots in fields of characteristic 2

anchor.snippet: In a field of characteristic 2, an element either has one square root or does not have any at all

annotations.note: Injectivity of Frobenius (`frobenius_inj`) implies in characteristic 2 the squaring map is injective, so square roots are unique when they exist; `isSquare_of_char_two` covers finite fields.

annotations.label: Quadratic residue

anchor.snippet: A non-zero element of the field

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `IsSquare` for quadratic-residue-style statements (e.g. `ZMod.isSquare_neg_one_iff`); there is no dedicated `QuadraticResidue` predicate.

annotations.label: Square roots of 1 modulo 8

anchor.snippet: the element 1 has four distinct square roots

annotations.note: No specific enumeration of the four square roots of 1 in `ZMod 8` is recorded.

annotations.label: Square roots of −1 in the quaternions

anchor.snippet: the element −1 has infinitely many square roots

annotations.note: Although `Quaternion` is formalized, the sphere of square roots of `-1` is not characterized explicitly.

anchors.snippet: the element −1 has infinitely many square roots

annotations.label: Square root of 0 in a ring

anchor.snippet: A square root of 0 is either 0 or a zero divisor

annotations.note: `mul_self_eq_zero` says `x*x = 0 ↔ x = 0` in a no-zero-divisors ring; the exact phrasing 'either 0 or a zero divisor' for general rings is not stated as a single lemma.

annotations.label: Geometric construction via similar triangles

anchor.section: Geometric construction of the square root
anchor.snippet: Euclid's second proof in Book VI depends on the theory of similar triangles

annotations.note: No Euclidean construction of the geometric mean/square root via similar triangles is formalized.

annotations.label: Iterative right-triangle construction (Spiral of Theodorus)

anchor.section: Geometric construction of the square root
anchor.snippet: Another method of geometric construction uses right triangles and induction

annotations.note: The Spiral of Theodorus construction has no formalization in Mathlib.
